title: Foreign aid given for emergencies and longer-term development
subtitle: Committed bilateral [official development assistance (ODA)](#dod:oda) from governments and multilateral organizations, grants from civil society organizations. Aid for emergencies, such as natural disasters and wars, is often called humanitarian aid. This data is expressed in US dollars and adjusted for inflation.
caption:
Foreign aid given for emergencies and longer-term development, DAC countries, 1971 to 2024

Committed bilateral official development assistance (ODA) from governments and multilateral organizations, grants from civil society organizations. Aid for emergencies, such as natural disasters and wars, is often called humanitarian aid. This data is expressed in US dollars and adjusted for inflation.

The area chart tracks committed bilateral official development assistance from 1971 through 2024, measuring financial flows in constant 2023 US dollars. The visualization stacks two distinct categories: a lower pink band representing aid for emergencies and a dominant upper green band representing aid for longer-term development. The vertical axis ranges from zero to $200 billion, allowing for a clear view of the total volume and the proportional split between the two types of assistance. Throughout the entire period, aid for longer-term development constitutes the vast majority of the total, consistently occupying the space above the emergency aid layer. In 1971, the total aid volume sits just above the $40 billion mark, with emergency aid forming a negligible sliver at the base. By 1990, a visible spike pushes the total near $100 billion, driven largely by the green development sector. The data shows a period of relative stability in the 1990s, hovering between $60 billion and $70 billion, before a sustained upward trajectory begins around the year 2000. Total aid crosses the $100 billion threshold in the mid-2000s and continues to climb, reaching a peak just above $180 billion around 2023 before a slight decline in 2024. The pink band for emergency aid remains relatively flat and low for decades, showing only a modest increase in absolute terms during the 2010s and 2020s, never exceeding $30 billion. The data is expressed in constant 2023 US dollars to account for inflation. The chart shows that while emergency assistance has grown slightly in recent years, the long-term trend is defined by the substantial expansion of development-focused funding.
